text: Laparoscopic Ventral Mesh Rectopexy Step by step, rectal prolapse is defined as protrusion of the entire thickness of the rectal wall through the anal sphincter. Classified into two types, mucosal prolapse and full thickness prolapse. Multiple risk factor is defined. The most important risk are chronic straining with defecation and constipation. The aim of treatment for rectal prolapse are Control prolapse and prevent recurrence. Restore normal bowel function in term of continence and constipation. Surgery can be split into two main approaches. Pirineal. And abdominal. Over one hundred procedure now described. But now there is no established consensus as to the best surgical treatment.

Laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy. This procedure aims to correct the descent of the posterior and middle pelvic compartments, combined with reinforcement of the rectovaginal septum. The operation has been shown to have low recurrence rate and also improves incontinence and constipation. The advantage of the laparoscopic approach are shorter hospital stay, quicker patient recovery, lower costs and less morbidity.

No history of constipation and incontinence. Gastroscope and colonoscope reveal normal. No staining evaluation in this case. Laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy is performed. Step-up procedure are shown in this slide.

The operative field is set up with the patient in the lithotomy position, arms tucked. The surgeon and camera operator are positioned on the right side, while the assistant is on the left. The camera is inserted through the umbilical port, and three additional 5mm ports are placed.

The right ureter is visualized crossing the right iliac artery. The assistant retracts the mesosigmoid ventrally, and a peritoneal incision is made over the sacral promontory. The sacral hollow is exposed for subsequent mesh fixation using blunt and sharp dissection.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the right hypogastric nerve. The rectovaginal septum is opened, and dissection proceeds between the rectum and the posterior vaginal wall. The lateral ligament is preserved to prevent recurrence and reduce constipation. The rectovaginal septum is opened broadly down to the pelvic floor.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision caudally into an inverted J shape along the right side of the rectum, curving it to the left over the ventral aspect of the rectum and the pouch of Douglas. A strip of prolene mesh, measuring three by fifteen centimeters, is inserted. Temporary hysteropexy is achieved with transparietal sutures, suspending the uterus to the abdominal wall using non-absorbable sutures. The mesh is then sutured to the ventral aspect on both sides of the distal rectum.

The mesh is positioned to reinforce the rectovaginal septum and is fixed onto the sacral promontory using a tacker. The surgeon then closes the lateral borders of the incised peritoneum over the mesh with resorbable sutures.

The mesh should be covered completely with peritoneum to avoid any later small bowel fixation to the mesh. There was no complication post-surgery. Patient can discharge on post-operative day four. There was no recurrent on four-month follow-up. No new developed constipation or incontinence. In conclusion, a variety of procedures is available for correction of rectal prolapse. Laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy appears to be a safe and effective surgical option for folicness rectal prolapse, in terms of intraoperative, postoperative, and mesh-related complications. with acceptable recurrence rate and satisfactory improvement of functional outcome Thank you for your attention
